is a programming methodology for embedded control systems running on possibly distributed platforms. The Giotto system consists of a time-triggered programming language, a compiler, and a runtime system. Giotto aims at hard real-time applicat

e system. Giotto aims at hard real-time applications with periodic behavior. The Giotto Language and Compiler The Giotto programming language provides an abstract programmer's model. Giotto strictly separates the platform-independent functional

ly separates the platform-independent functionality and timing concerns from platform-dependent scheduling and communication issues. The time-triggered predictability of Giotto makes it particularly suitable for safety-critical applications with

ly suitable for safety-critical applications with hard real-time constraints. The Runtime System The runtime system is composed of two virtual machines. The first one is called Embedded Machine (E Machine) and handles the interaction with the envir

hine) and handles the interaction with the environment (reactive). It interprets E code, which supervises the execution of software tasks in response to physical events. The other one is the Scheduling Machine (S Machine) and handles the intera

uling Machine (S Machine) and handles the interaction with the running platform (proactive). It interprets S code, which specifies the temporal order of task execution. Figure 1 shows how E and S machine interact with the physical environment,

S machine interact with the physical environment, software tasks, and hardware platform. Fig 1. E Machine and S Machine The Giotto project has been supported by the Air Force Office of Scientific Research, by the California MICRO program, by DARPA, b

arch, by the California MICRO program, by DARPA, by the National Science Foundation, and by Wind River Systems. <!-- OLD TEXT What is Giotto? Giotto is a programming methodology for embedded control systems running on possibly distributed platfor

ol systems running on possibly distributed platforms. Giotto consists of a time-triggered programming language, a compiler, and a runtime library. Giotto aims at hard real-time applications with periodic behavior. With the explosion of the market

dic behavior. With the explosion of the market for embedded appliances there is an increasing demand for high-level design methodologies for embedded systems. Embedded programming is a complex task which involves concurrency handling under real-ti

which involves concurrency handling under real-time constraints. Target platforms may be distributed and are typically characterized by severe performance constraints. However, the achievements in improving software quality and reuse potential throu

proving software quality and reuse potential through high-level programming languages and automatic compilation do not easily carry over to embedded software. Today's embedded software design practice is still close to the level of non-embedded progr

is still close to the level of non-embedded programming of the late sixties. Modifications of the embedded platform configuration typically require complete re-implementation and validation. The challenge to improving embedded software design is

hallenge to improving embedded software design is to understand the nature of the synthesis of real-time and concurrency and its operational counterparts, real-time scheduling and process communication. We propose a tool-supported design methodology

n. We propose a tool-supported design methodology called Giotto for implementing embedded control systems on platforms of possibly distributed sensors, actuators, CPUs, and networks. Giotto consists of a time-triggered programming language which prov

f a time-triggered programming language which provides an abstract programmer's model, as well as a compiler and a runtime library which can be targeted toward various platforms. Giotto supports the automation of the control system design process by

utomation of the control system design process by strictly separating platform-independent functionality and timing concerns from platform-dependent scheduling and communication issues. The time-triggered predictability of Giotto makes it particularl

ered predictability of Giotto makes it particularly suitable for safety-critical applications with hard real-time constraints. Future objectives in the Giotto project are formal verification of real-time properties of Giotto programs using a refin

l-time properties of Giotto programs using a refinement relation to the modeling language Masaccio. The Giotto project at the University of California at Berkeley is funded in part by the DARPA SEC grant F33615-C-98-3614, the MARCO GSRC grant 98-D

grant F33615-C-98-3614, the MARCO GSRC grant 98-DT-660, the AFOSR MURI grant F49620-00-1-0327, the California MICRO grant 01-037, and the NSF grants CCR-0208875, CCR-0085949, and CCR-0225610.
